title: Today's AI Brief: Gemini 3.5 and The New York Times' AI Fight
summary: Google's new Gemini 3.5 model and The New York Times' union dispute over AI monitoring
imageAlt: Image from source article for Gemini 3.5: frontier intelligence with action

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to Today's AI Brief. I've got a couple of interesting stories to share with you today. First up, Google has announced its latest AI model, Gemini 3.5, which promises to bring frontier intelligence with action. This new model is designed to help execute complex workflows, and I'm excited to see how it performs in real-world scenarios.

According to Google's blog post, Gemini 3.5 is built to help users execute complex, agentic workflows. This means it can handle tasks that require multiple steps and decisions, making it a significant upgrade from previous models. For instance, imagine a manufacturing plant that needs to optimize its production line - Gemini 3.5 could potentially help streamline the process and improve efficiency. My read is that this could be a game-changer for industries that rely on automation and process optimization.

Next, I came across an interesting article from The Verge about a dispute brewing inside The New York Times. Unionized tech employees at the Times claim that the company is violating their contract by using AI tools to monitor employee performance. It seems that the union is pushing back against the company's increasing reliance on AI, and I think this raises important questions about the boundaries between technology and human oversight. As AI becomes more prevalent in the workplace, it's natural that concerns about job security and fairness arise.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.

sources.title: The AI fight brewing inside The New York Times
